text: Today we will be presenting a case of a robot-assisted partial nephrectomy in an ectopic pelvic kidney. Pelvic kidneys result from an incomplete or aberrant ascent during embryologic development. They are a rare occurrence and present special treatment challenges due to a greater risk of injury of aberrant vessels or abdominal viscera.

The patient was a sixty-nine year old male who was referred for an incidental renal mass. He had no prior abdominal surgeries and had normal renal function. A CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed a four point one centimeter mass in the lower pole suspicious for renal cell carcinoma in the left pelvic kidney as well as a duplication of the infrarenal IVC. A left renal artery arises off of the aortoiliac confluence. An accessory renal artery is seen arising off of the proximal left common iliac artery and traces along the lateral aspect of the kidney. Treatment options were discussed, and the patient elected to undergo a robot-assisted partial nephrectomy of this mass.

The patient is positioned supine on a non-slip mat with arms tucked. The surgical team completes standard preparation and draping. Eight millimeter robotic trocars are inserted higher in the abdomen, following a pelvic surgery approach, along with a twelve millimeter air seal and five millimeter assistant ports.

The surgeon enters the abdomen using a varus needle technique and positions the patient in steep Trendelenburg, moving the bowels cranially. The kidney is identified in the pelvis. Under direct vision, additional ports are placed, and instruments are advanced. Blunt dissection and monopolar cautery are used to reflect the bowel, exposing the kidney. Dissection along the medial aspect of the kidney reveals the hilum, where the renal artery and vein are identified in their cranial location, along with an additional renal artery from the common iliac. The vessels are carefully dissected for future clamping.

Hemostasis is achieved using bipolar cautery and sponges. The surgeon exposes the renal mass by dissecting Girota's fascia. Intraoperative ultrasound confirms the mass's location. During dissection on the medial aspect, the ureter is visualized wrapping around the mass and is carefully skeletonized and mobilized medially.

The surgeon mobilizes the kidney, exposing the surface of the renal mass. Intraoperative ultrasound is used to demarcate the boundaries between the mass and normal renal parenchyma. Both renal arteries are clamped with curved bulldog clamps. The surgeon excises the mass, identifying a plane between the tumor and normal tissue, and encounters a renal cyst, which serves as a landmark.
The surgeon completes the excision of the renal mass, ensuring thorough hemostasis. The base of the resection is over-sewn with 2-0 Monocryl suture, which is secured with Hemolock clips.

The surgeon removes the clamps from the renal arteries after eight minutes of clamping. Adequate hemostasis is assessed before closing the renal capsule using a 2-0 V-Lock suture, secured with Hemolock clips after each throw.
The surgeon cuts both layers of sutures and removes the needles. The excised specimen is then placed into an endocatch bag for retrieval.

The surgeon irrigates and examines the surgical bed to ensure hemostasis, reinforcing it with hemostatic agents. Girota's fascia is closed using two OV-lock sutures. The specimen is removed, and the surgical incisions are closed in layers.
The patient's post-operative course was uncomplicated and he was discharged home in stable condition on post-op day one. Pathology showed a four point five centimeter papillary renal cell carcinoma.

Robotic-assisted partial nephrectomy in patients with complex anatomy require adherence to the same basic surgical principles as any other surgery, including detailed preoperative planning of the optimal approach and port placements, meticulous dissection of hilar structures, and adequate exposure of the entirety of the mass. Some key steps that allowed us to successfully complete the surgery were to obtain appropriate preoperative imaging and the use of intraoperative ultrasound to complete sufficient mobilization of the kidney, which was achieved by optimal use of the fourth arm and the surgical assistant.
